From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music without vocals

For other uses, see [Instrumental (disambiguation)](/wiki/Instrumental_(disambiguation) "Instrumental (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Instrumental&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(November 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

An **instrumental**, **instrumental music**, or sometimes **"instrumental song"** is [music](/wiki/Music "Music") without any [vocals](/wiki/Vocal_music "Vocal music"), although it might include some inarticulate vocals, such as shouted [backup vocals](/wiki/Backup_vocal "Backup vocal") in a [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") setting. Through [semantic widening](/wiki/Semantic_change "Semantic change"), a broader sense of the word "[song](/wiki/Song "Song")" may refer to instrumentals.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The music is primarily or exclusively produced using [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"). An instrumental can exist in [music notation](/wiki/Music_notation "Music notation"), after it is written by a [composer](/wiki/Composer "Composer"); in the mind of one or more composers in question (especially in cases where he/they will perform the piece, as in the case of a [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") solo guitarist or a [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") fiddle player); as a piece that is performed live by a single instrumentalist or a [musical ensemble](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble"), which could range in components from a [duo](/wiki/Duet "Duet") or [trio](/wiki/Trio_(music) "Trio (music)") to a large big band, [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band") or [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra").

In a song that is otherwise sung, a section that is not sung but which is played by instruments can be called an **instrumental interlude**, or, if it occurs at the beginning of the song, before the singer starts to sing, an instrumental [introduction](/wiki/Introduction_(music) "Introduction (music)"). If the instrumental section highlights the skill, musicality, and often the virtuosity of one or more particular performers, the section may be called a "solo" (e.g., the [guitar solo](/wiki/Guitar_solo "Guitar solo") that is a key section of [heavy metal music](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") and [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") songs). If the instruments are [percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") ones, the interlude can be called a percussion interlude or "percussion break". These interludes are a form of [break](/wiki/Break_(music) "Break (music)") in the song.

## In popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Instrumental&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In popular music")]

"Theme Q" by Steve Combs & Delta Is: an instrumental piece played on bass, drum, guitar, keyboard (4 min 53 s)

In commercial [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"), instrumental tracks are sometimes renderings, [remixes](/wiki/Remix "Remix") of a corresponding release that features vocals, but they may also be compositions originally conceived without vocals. One example of a genre in which both vocal/instrumental and solely instrumental songs are produced is blues. A [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") band often uses mostly songs that have lyrics that are sung, but during the band's show, they may also perform instrumental songs which only include [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [harmonica](/wiki/Harmonica "Harmonica"), [upright bass](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass")/[electric bass](/wiki/Electric_bass "Electric bass") and [drum kit](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit"). Instrumental versions of songs can also be used to create remixes and [mashups](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") or used in [DJ sets](/wiki/DJ_mix "DJ mix"). If an instrumental version of a track is not released, it can be created through [stem separation](/wiki/Stem_separation "Stem separation")/vocal removal.

## Borderline cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Instrumental&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Borderline cases")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Instrumental "Special:EditPage/Instrumental") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Some recordings which include brief or non-musical use of the human voice are typically considered instrumentals. Examples include songs with the following:

* Short verbal interjections (as in "[Tequila](/wiki/Tequila_(Champs_song) "Tequila (Champs song)")", "[Topsy](/wiki/Topsy_(instrumental) "Topsy (instrumental)")", "[Wipe Out](/wiki/Wipe_Out_(instrumental) "Wipe Out (instrumental)")", "[The Hustle](/wiki/The_Hustle_(song) "The Hustle (song)")", or "[Bentley's Gonna Sort You Out](/wiki/Bentley_Rhythm_Ace_(album) "Bentley Rhythm Ace (album)")")
* Repetitive [nonsense](/wiki/Non-lexical_vocables_in_music "Non-lexical vocables in music") words (e.g., "la la..." (as in "[Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta_(song) "Calcutta (song)")") or "[Woo Hoo](/wiki/Woo-Hoo_(Rock-A-Teens_song) "Woo-Hoo (Rock-A-Teens song)")")
* Non-musical spoken passages in the background of the track (e.g., "[To Live Is to Die](/wiki/To_Live_Is_to_Die "To Live Is to Die")" by [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica") or "[Wasteland](/wiki/Desolation_of_Eden "Desolation of Eden")" by [Chelsea Grin](/wiki/Chelsea_Grin "Chelsea Grin"))
* Wordless vocal effects, such as [drones](/wiki/Drone_(music) "Drone (music)") (e.g., "[Rockit](/wiki/Rockit_(song) "Rockit (song)")" or "[Flying](/wiki/Flying_(Beatles_instrumental) "Flying (Beatles instrumental)")")
* [Vocal percussion](/wiki/Vocal_percussion "Vocal percussion"), such as beatbox [B-sides](/wiki/B-sides "B-sides") on rap singles
* [Yelling](/wiki/Screaming_(music) "Screaming (music)") (e.g. "[Cry for a Shadow](/wiki/Cry_for_a_Shadow "Cry for a Shadow")")
* [Yodeling](/wiki/Yodeling "Yodeling") (e.g., "[Hocus Pocus](/wiki/Hocus_Pocus_(song) "Hocus Pocus (song)")")
* [Whistling](/wiki/Whistling "Whistling") (e.g., "[I Was Kaiser Bill's Batman](/wiki/I_Was_Kaiser_Bill%27s_Batman "I Was Kaiser Bill's Batman")" or "[Colonel Bogey March](/wiki/Colonel_Bogey_March "Colonel Bogey March")")
* Spoken statements at the end of the track (e.g., "[God Bless the Children of the Beast](/wiki/God_Bless_the_Children_of_the_Beast "God Bless the Children of the Beast")" by [Mötley Crüe](/wiki/M%C3%B6tley_Cr%C3%BCe "Mötley Crüe") or "[For the Love of God](/wiki/For_the_Love_of_God_(instrumental) "For the Love of God (instrumental)")" by [Steve Vai](/wiki/Steve_Vai "Steve Vai"))
* Non-musical vocal recordings taken from other media (e.g., "[Vampires](/wiki/Vampires_(Godsmack_song) "Vampires (Godsmack song)")" by [Godsmack](/wiki/Godsmack "Godsmack"))
* [Field recordings](/wiki/Field_recording "Field recording") which may or may not contain non-lyrical words (e.g., many songs by [Godspeed You! Black Emperor](/wiki/Godspeed_You!_Black_Emperor "Godspeed You! Black Emperor") and other [post-rock](/wiki/Post-rock "Post-rock") bands)

Songs including actual musical—rhythmic, melodic, and lyrical—vocals might still be categorized as instrumentals if the vocals appear only as a short part of an extended piece (e.g., "[Unchained Melody](/wiki/Unchained_Melody "Unchained Melody")" (Les Baxter), "[Batman Theme](/wiki/Batman_Theme "Batman Theme")", "[TSOP (The Sound of Philadelphia)](/wiki/TSOP_(The_Sound_of_Philadelphia) "TSOP (The Sound of Philadelphia)")", "[Pick Up the Pieces](/wiki/Pick_Up_the_Pieces_(Average_White_Band_song) "Pick Up the Pieces (Average White Band song)")", "The Hustle", "[Fly, Robin, Fly](/wiki/Fly,_Robin,_Fly "Fly, Robin, Fly")", "[Get Up and Boogie](/wiki/Get_Up_and_Boogie_(song) "Get Up and Boogie (song)")", "[Do It Any Way You Wanna](/wiki/Do_It_Any_Way_You_Wanna "Do It Any Way You Wanna")", and "[Gonna Fly Now](/wiki/Gonna_Fly_Now "Gonna Fly Now")"), though this definition is loose and subjective.

Falling just outside of that definition is "[Theme from *Shaft*](/wiki/Theme_from_Shaft "Theme from Shaft")" by [Isaac Hayes](/wiki/Isaac_Hayes "Isaac Hayes").

"[Better Off Alone](/wiki/Better_Off_Alone "Better Off Alone")", which began as an instrumental by [DJ Jurgen](/wiki/DJ_Jurgen "DJ Jurgen"), had vocals by [Judith Pronk](/wiki/Judith_Pronk "Judith Pronk"), who would become a seminal part of [Alice Deejay](/wiki/Alice_Deejay "Alice Deejay"), added in later releases of the track.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Instrumental&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-oh_mein_papa_7-0)** Contains several vocal interjections of the title.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-tequila_11-0)** Features vocal interjections of the title at the end of each chorus.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-hoots_mon_12-0)** Contains several Scottish-sounding grunts at the end of each chorus and immediately beforehand.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-stranger_on_the_shore_21-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-stranger_on_the_shore_21-1) [Stranger on the Shore](/wiki/Stranger_on_the_Shore "Stranger on the Shore") hit #1 on the end of year UK charts, but NOT the weekly UK charts. Despite this, it is the highest selling instrumental single worldwide and in the UK; in the US, this honor falls to [Meco](/wiki/Meco "Meco")'s [Star Wars Theme/Cantina Band](/wiki/Star_Wars_Theme/Cantina_Band "Star Wars Theme/Cantina Band").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-mouldy_old_dough_27-0)** Contains vocal interjections before, during, and immediately after the choruses.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-tsop_29-0)** Contains vocals at the beginning and during the fade-out.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-pick_up_the_pieces_30-0)** Contains vocal interjections at the end of the second and third verses.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-the_hustle_31-0)** Contains vocal interjections of "do the hustle!" at the end of each chorus.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-fly_robin_fly_32-0)** Contains vocal interjections of the title at the end of each chorus and "up, up to the sky" as an ending.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-gonna_fly_now_33-0)** Contains vocals, which total thirty words and thus contains the most lyrics of any song classified as an instrumental which has hit number 1.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-beyond_34-0)** Includes spoken introduction, and vocal interjections of the song title and "Here we go" at several points during the song.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-doop_35-0)** Contains, during its choruses, several nonsensical vocal interjections of the title.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-flat_beat_37-0)** At the beginning, before the main piece begins, it features the lyrics "Oh yeah, I used to know Quentin, he's a real, he's a real jerk".
14. **[^](#cite_ref-bromance_39-0)** Bromance was an instrumental before being re-released as "Seek Bromance" with vocals by Amanda Wilson from the song "Love U Seek" by Italian DJ Samuele Sartini.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-harlem_shake_40-0)** Contains samples of the lines "Con los terroristas" from a remix of the 2006 reggaeton single "Maldades" by Héctor Delgado and "Do the Harlem shake" from "Miller Time" by Plastic Little.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-animals_41-0)** "We're the fucking animals" is said twice.
